does: This paper introduces the Joint Sparse Autoencoder (JSAE), a method that extends sparse autoencoders to vision-language models by enforcing explicit alignment constraints between vision and language modalities. JSAE factorizes sequence-pooled activations from both modalities into shared, interpretable features at the image and caption level. The authors apply JSAE to LLaVA and other VLMs, recovering cross-modal features corresponding to recognizable concepts like food and animals. Through bidirectional interventions—both additive steering and suppression—they characterize how effectively these features control model behavior across different layers, finding that additive steering peaks at mid-to-late layers while suppression effects remain relatively consistent, though within statistical noise, across all probed layers.
connects: This work builds directly on the success of sparse autoencoders for interpreting and steering language models, extending this mechanistic interpretability approach to the multi-modal setting. It responds to the challenge that standard SAE factorizations of VLM activations typically fail to yield interpretable, controllable cross-modal directions. The paper draws on prior work using interventions and steering in language models, adapting these techniques to jointly model vision and language. The alignment constraint approach is motivated by the need to discover shared representations across modalities, building on broader research into vision-language model alignment and the interaction between vision and language components in models like LLaVA.
enables: This work opens avenues for more interpretable and controllable vision-language models by establishing mechanistic understanding of cross-modal features and their causal roles in different layers. The layer-dependent asymmetry findings suggest that future steering interventions could be more precisely targeted, improving the design of alignment and safety techniques for VLMs. The method provides tools for auditing and understanding what multimodal concepts VLMs learn and how they influence downstream outputs, which is valuable for making VLMs more transparent and controllable. This foundation could enable better approaches to steering vision-language behavior, addressing safety concerns, and developing more interpretable multimodal systems.
